Introduction Migration models are often constructed to predict the scale, composition, distance, length of the migratory movement and consequences of such movements. The internal migra-tion consists one of the major problems that a geographical region faces during the transition process from the tradition-al way of production to the modern one, so it attracts wide attention in migration literature (Papelias 2013). The study of migration in general, and rural-urban migration, in par-ticular, has for long been an important area of research in development economics. The Haris-Todaro framework ac-cording to Zenou (2010) has become a cornerstone of models of rural-urban migration. The aim of the Haris-Todaro framework is to explain the persistent rural-urban migration in developing countries despite the high unemployment rates in the cities. The main idea is quite simple since it says that migration will occur as long as the urban expected income is higher than the rural one. The original model has been extended in different direc-tions to incorporate different aspects of labor markets and migration in less developed countries (Zenou 2010). Other models based on push- pull theories see the city as Basil U. Eze Basil U. Eze Arejoen Vol 4(2) 2021, 22-28 23 a magnet of opportunity but fails to explain many intricacies in the migration process Witman posited out that push and pull factors are one and the same, together they provide the perception of difference between “here” and “there” (Witman 1975). This means therefore according Mc Donald and de Haan (2003), that they have no heuristic val-ue. Place- utility and other micro- theories are weakened by the assumption that decision- makers have perfect knowledge about the costs and benefits of migration. Clear-ly, this is not the case, people act with limited and often flawed knowledge. The theory of migration that prevailed in the antiquity, con-centrated its main concern to the pull factor. Traditionally this was established as the classical theory. The case of the poor peasant going to town in order to “find a better future” depicts the rough scene of the theory. The “city light”, the thrilling life, the fast and easy creation of wealth, the freedom that anonymity offers, the easiness of establishing business are some of the most important cases related to urbanism (Papelias 2013). The migration of labour from rural to urban areas is an important part of the urbani-zation process in developing countries especially Nigeria. There is no point stressing that it has been the focus of abundant research over many decades now, some key poli-cy issues are still cloudy and the existing theoretical models equally posit conflicting policy implications, for example one of the conclusion of the Harris-Todaro Model is that creating urban jobs is an insufficient solution to the urban unemployment problem because of the induced negative effect on rural migration which outweigh the positive effect of creating jobs-So called the Todaro paradox (Zenou 2010). The crux of this literature is therefore to survey some of the basic existing theoretical models, examine some of the poli-cy implications and see how these can be adopted and adapted as policy option to enhance urban and rural devel-opment in Nigeria. This paper is therefore discussed in two major parts. The first part reviews some of major existing theoretical models which are categorized into Non-economic theories, Economic theories Integrative theories and Recent theoretical frameworks (Eze 2020). The second section examines their policy implications and how they can be adapted to enhance regional development in Nigeria while the last section concludes. Major Existing Theoretical ModelsSince Raveinstein’s seminal work on migration, several, theories have been put forward in an attempt to explain why some people migrate from one place to another; what determines the choice of destination of migrants, as well’ as why others do not move at all, even though they locate within the same sources re-gion with the former. Some view these theories as dichoto-mized into two, name1y: The non-economic theories and the economic theories. However, in this paper extra two theories are recognized namely: the integrative theories and recent theoretical frameworks bringing the total to four as listed below (Eze, 2020) The non-economic theories of migration The economic theories of migration The integrative theories The recent theoretical frameworks 2.1Non-economic theories of migration The simplest of the non-economic theories according to Peterson (1975) takes account of distance as the major con-trolling factor, number of migrants being inversely related to distance. The gravity models according to Johnston (1970), as cited by Eze (2014) in his theory of intervening oppor-tunities saw the volume of migration over a given dis-tance as directly proportional to the number of opportuni-ties at the point of destination but inversely proportional to the number of opportunities between the point of de-parture and the destination. Morrill and Pitt (1967), in their idea of mean information field, held that the destina-tion of most movers seem to be governed by the interplay of distance and information. This information consists of the knowledge an individual has of his world and his in-formation field is the spatial distribution of the domains of such knowledge which can then be used to predict the volume and pattern of the movement. 2.2 Economic theories of migration Much weight of migration theories leans towards the eco-nomic theories which are used to explain the economic motive of migration especially between rural and urban areas. The economic theories of migration view migrants as rationally optimizing the costs and benefits of their decision to migrate. Among the leading exponents of the economic theories are Lewis; Sjaastad and Todaro (Eze 2014). According to Lall, Selod and Shalizi (2006), the first the-oretical work involving rural to urban migration is the lewis (1954) model of development which tries to explain the transition from a stagnating economy based on a tradi-tional rural sector to a growing economy driven by the development of a modern urban sector. In this model, economic growth does not only stem from the accumula-tion of capital in modern industry but also from the inter-action between the rural and urban sectors especially the issue of labour transfer between the two economic sectors through migration from rural to urban areas. Thus, lewis, extended by the works of Ranis and Fei (1961), examined the processes of rural to urban labour transfer within the context of the dual economy of the developing nations and the existence of labour surplus in the rural sector of these nations. In this case the migrant avails himself of the range of opportunities not easily available in rural areas (Eze,1991). Both the Lewis and Ranis and Fei formalizations describe in a very stylized manner a general mechanism occurring at an initial stage of economic development, which justi-fies the assumed scarcity of capital and the abundance of labour in these models. The salient note in this model is that the rural sector can massively provide migrants to the urban sector. In this simple framework, internal migration is desirable to the extent that it accompanied growth (Lall et al,2006). The hypothesis of Sjaastad (1962), focuses on the costs and benefits approach to migration to account for the in-dividuals’ decision to migrate. According to this model people will move when the net monetary and non-monetary benefits of migration are greater than the costs. Specifically, relevant to the African context is the hypoth-esis of Todaro (1969) which suggest that migration will continue as long as rural income levels are less than “expected” urban income levels. Thus Todaro (1969) and Harris-Todaro (1970) models equally consider the role of internal migration in a dual economy in which the urban sector draws labor force from the rural sector. Later, in his summary of the economic interpretation, Todaro in 1976 as cited by Eze (1991), suggests that migration is positively correlated among others, with the desire for economic gain, rural-urban expected income differential; 24 that migration is stimulated primarily by rational economic consideration of relative benefits and costs of both financial and psychological nature. Thus Todaro’s proposition is a simple dynamic formalization in which individual migration decisions are based on the difference between discounted expected income streams in urban and rural areas net of migration costs. In this model, an urban job-seeker evaluates his discounted expected income stream in the city taking into account the endogenous probability of being employed. (Lall et al 2006). Looking at Lewis model and the Harris-Todaro model, the difference is in a radical change of focus. In Lewis model, internal migration removed “disguised un-employment” from rural areas and enabled the transition to a modern economy. In Todarian models, the focus is on explaining the existence of unemployment in urban areas and its link with internal migration. The main contribution of Todaro’s framework is thus to link urban unemployment and migration flows. Under certain parameter values, the intensity of the link can lead to a para-dox (known as the Todaro paradox) a policy aiming at in-creasing the number of available jobs in a city may very well increase the level of unemployment because of induced migration. The major criticism of the economic model is the assump-tion that a prospective migrant will be in a position to evalu-ate the probability of getting an urban job-which overstates the level of communication and information about the urban labour market available to the rural resident in the develop-ing countries in general. In most cases, rural migrants espe-cially potential wage seekers, have moved to towns because of the presence of relations or contacts at destination and the hope that these contacts could help them secure employment rather than on any positive assurance of getting substantial or sustained urban employment (Fadayomi, 1988). A further critic of the economic models as typified by the neoclassical work of Todaro’s model is made by medola (2006). Accord-ing to him, despite its seminal contribution to understanding people outflow, this approach has failed to account for the risk nature of migration and empirical evidence showing that people’s movement does not equilibrate expected in-come acrçss regions (Kartz and Stark, 1986; Rosenzweig, 1988). Indeed, the main limitation of the Todaro model is that it does not include any other influence besides expected income, that shape potential migrants’ decision and also potential impacts on source economics. Furthermore, it fails to explain temporary migration and the substantial flow of remittances from migrants to people at the source (Taylor and Martin, 2001). These issues, on the other hand, are the most pervasive features of out-migration phenomena, espe-cially from rural to urban areas. The perspective that migration is not driven by labour mar-ket imperfections only, but by a variety of market failures, including missing or incomplete capital and insurance mar-ket, is a trademark of the more recent new economics of labour Migration (NELM) (Stark and Bloom, 1985; Stark, 1991) A further novelty of the later approach is that migra-tion decisions are viewed as taking place within a larger context than the domain of isolated individuals, typically the household or families. Also the adverse economic position of households at community levels (their ‘relative depriva-tion’) influences the household behaviour with respect to migration (Stark et al; 1986; Stark an4 Taylor, 1987,). The NELM approach conceives migration as a family strategy whereby migrants and resident household members act col-lectively not only to maximize income, but also to minimize risks, diversify income earnings and loosen financial con-straints through remittance (Taylor, 1996). Migrants and household members at origin maintain connection of fam-ily loyalty, exchange of transfers and parental asset pool-ing (Stark and Levbhari, 1982). It follows that, according to the NELM approach, migration is conceived in terms of risk management, income diversification and allevia-tion of liquidity constraints at household level (Medola, 2006). 2.3The ‘Integrative’ Theories Other theories of migration as the “push-pull” theory put forth by lee in 1966, “Bright Light theory” put forth by Gulliver in 1955 plus systems approach to the theory of rural-urban migration conceptualized by mabogunje in 1970 and theory of migration as an innovation by Findley (1979) plus the network and cumulative causation theo-ries have made some efforts at integrating the social and economic factors in migration decision. In the push-pull theory, the factors which generally prompt migration can be classified into “Push” and “pull” factors which are correspondingly repulsive and attractive forces. The push factors normally include deteriorating socio-economic conditions in the origin and which literal-ly force people to move out of such locations. The pull factors include the attractions and socio-economic oppor-tunities available in other localities –attractions which are sufficiently powerful to not only attract people to that location, but more importantly retain people within it. Lee equally identified the intervening factors which act as a strong filter which affect many potential moves such fac-tors which may hinder moving include distance, cost, natural barriers (hills, water bodies), social structures such as leadership position or influential title in the fami-ly or village etc. The “bright light theory” of Gulliver tried to integrate attractiveness of the city life to economic factors. Here the attractiveness of the city is the major determinant that lures the migrants to move. In this case the decision to migrate is essentially made by the individual rather than the household. According to the systems approach, rural-urban migration is influenced by the level of integration of rural communities into the national economy. There-fore, with increased economic development, improvement in transportation and communication links, villagers’ awareness of the prospects of the urban centres for them-selves and their children increase. In the process, the rural family and the village community act as the rural control subsystem which receives and reacts to migration stimuli arising from the interaction of several rural and urban adjustment factors. On the hand, the urban control subsys-tem consists of the city administration and voluntary or-ganizations which operate at the opposite end to encour-age or discourage the potential migrants. The rural-urban migration in this context is therefore seen as an open sys-tem (Eze, 2014a). The systems approach is a major at-tempt at viewing the migration process holistically. The theory of migration as an innovation expects a shift in migrant type and the relative importance of causal factors as a nation passes from one migration urbanization types to another ie through adoption, diffusion, dispersal to the last phase of “innovatively staying” in the rural areas. It is reported many years ago that Nigeria has already reached the diffusion stage of migration-urbanization where mi-gration as an innovation is been adopted by many house-holds due to improved information about urban prospects and the presence of extended family members and fellow villagers at chosen destinations. These two approaches by 25 Mabogunje and Findley actually provide a reasonable framework for understanding migration in Africa and have considerably modified the shortcomings of the economic model. The network theory of migration highlights the role of social relationship in fostering migration phenomena (Boyd, 1989). Interpersonal ties, such as kinship, friendship and shared community origin, between migrants and non-migrants in origin and destination areas are likely to in-crease the likelihood to migrate (at individual and household levels). This is so because this form of social capital lowers the costs and risks of movement and increases the expected net-returns to migration (Massay et al, 1993). Furthermore, in the cumulative causation theory, migration is conceived as a dynamic and cumulative phenomenon in that, when it occurs, socioeconomic contexts at origin are altered in the way they lead to further migration. In this sense, the commonly observed effect of social networks raises the likelihood of the next wave of potential migrants electing to move, enhancing geographic concentration of migrant origins. According to the theory, ultimately, the self -perpetuating nature of migration may overcome the eco-nomic motivations that originated it, reducing the number of control variable for migration policy concerns (Massey et al, 1993). On the whole, disparity in socio economic development biased in favour of urban areas remains the controlling force of rural out-migration to urban areas in Nigeria and several areas of the developing world. 2.4 Recent Theoretical frameworks of Determinants of migration Previously, migration literature has distinguished between three types of determinants; the root causes; proximate con-ditions; and intervening factors (Schmeidl, 1997). While proximate conditions of migration capture factors that are closely linked to the actual migratory move in respect to timing such as intensity of violence, root causes are associ-ated with more underlying longer-term factors, such as pov-erty or employment opportunities and wages. Here, factors such as political factors are generally defined as proxi-mate conditions, whereas root causes are mainly of eco-nomic nature. The intervening factors were added to this framework at a later point and are based on the notion of facilitating or hindering factors of migration, such as net-works or “migration culture.” (Kuhnt 2019). Timmerman, Heyse and van mol (2010) proposed a theoretical frame-work in which the causes and drivers of migration are systematically studied. This framework structures the determinants of migration according to three different levels: The macro, meso and micro-level. These then in-fluence the perceptions and aspirations of potential mi-grants and jointly form their emigration environment. In contrast to the majority of existent frameworks and theo-ries, Tummerman et al (2010), account simultaneously for origin and destination factors and consider their effect on migration aspirations. This their framework is actually inspired by carling’s (2002) definition of the emigration environment, which is influenced by the social, political and economic context and which sees migration as a so-cially and culturally constructed project with micro and macro-level influences Timmerman et al (2010) and Tim-merman, De Clerk, Hemmerechts, and Willems (2014) extend these ideas and establish the emigration environ-ment in which perceptions and the migration aspirations are formed by macro-meso and micro level determinants and within which the decision to move is taken. Kuhnt (2019) in his study of factors that influence peo-ples’ decision to move extend the ideas equally by adding interactions between the three levels (f1g.1). The im-portance of a macro-level factor-such as economic oppor-tunities-is likely to be sensitive to meso-level factors such as networks (that can facilitate jobs) or to micro-level factors, such as educational level (affecting employ-ability). This stresses the complexity of factors at hand, which cannot be unilaterally linked to the categories of migratory movements. The interaction of factors subse-quently affects the perceptions and aspirations of a poten-tial migrant. 26 The macro-level refers to factors that are common to all potential migrants in a particular country, such as the socio-economic and political context or migration governance and policies of origin and destination countries, regions or other entities (for instance, EU (European Union) mandates). The meso-level encompasses sub-national or local factors, in-cluding networks or cultural reasons. Individual and house-hold characteristics of potential migrants, such as gender, age, educational level, and social status, as well as behav-ioural factors, such as risk aversion, are part of the micro-level. The framework does not suggest a hierarchy between the three different levels. In the literature, it has been put for-ward that macro-level factors play a dominant role, while meso- and micro-level determinants tend to be seen as inter-mediaries. While it is also recognized that macro-level fac-tors are highly important and micro-level factors are less of a cause and rather a mediator, a general hierarchy across levels is, however, still up to empirics to decide (kuhnt 2019). Apart from the variety of determinants that influence a per-son’s perception and aspirations to migrate, it is also, im-portant to consider their capability to actually do so. People can only move if they have the appropriate agency and op-portunity structures at their disposal (de Haas et al., 2018; Timmerman et al, 2014). They need to have access to social, human and financial capital to realise their migration aspira-tions (de Haas, 2010; de; Haas et al., 2018). It has been in-creasingly noted that when studying migratory movements, one should consider people’s capabilities and aspirations jointly (Carling. 2002; de Haas, 2003). These are influenced at the micro-, meso- and macro-levels and only once they are taken into account might one be able to explain the indi-vidual differences seen in the migraton reality. Capabilities and aspirations are not equally distributed across societies and are subject to change over time and place as they are inter alia influenced by the determinants at the three differ-ent levels, which are not constant (Timmerman et at., 2014; Van Mol, Snel, Hemmerechts, & Timmerman, 2018). Policy Options for Regional Development The study of migration in general and rural urban migration in particular has for long been an important area of research in development economics. The Harris-Todaro Framework became a cornerstone of models of rural-urban migration. The aim of the Harris-Todaro framework is to explain the persistent rural-urban migration in developing countries despite the high unemployment rates in the cities. However, the important issue in this section is to examine the policy implictations of these models generally. Apath from its apparent consistency with stylized facts (reconciling internal migration and urban unemployment), another feature of the Harris-Todaro model is to lend itself to a simple welfare analysis showing that the market equi-librium is suboptimal thereby providing direction for gov-ernment interventions (lall et al 2006). According to them, this suboptimality justifies policy recommendations of two kinds in order to improve welfare: acting on migration and/ or wages. Understanding that there is excess migration; the question is how to best attenuate the urban unemployment problem without encouraging migration from rural to urban areas-overcoming the Todora paradox. The Todora paradox conveyed the message that internal migration can be harm-ful because it exacerbates urban unemployment. Given the high unemployment rates and significant migration to cities in developing countries, this idea has certainly inspired many governments to implement restrictive policies yet the empirical validity of the model and the paradox is yet clearly established ( lall et al 2006). The way out of this dilemma, is that instead of imple-menting one-way restrictive policy, we can pursue a poli-cy that simultaneously increases urban employment and reduces rural-urban migration. Another possibility would be to combine two policies. For example, one could sub-sidy the employment, not only in the city (creating urban jobs) but also in the rural area (deterring rural-urban mi-gration). (Zenou,2010, Eze 2014) Going for an outright policies of restricting rural- urban migration could prove very harmful. Mobility is a human right and depriving people of that right is difficult to justi-fy. The only economic justification for restricting migra-tion flows would be that migrants do not internalize the possible negative externalities they exert. In any case, this judgment would not require an eradication of migration but a regulation of the phenomenon to bring it to a social-ly desirable level (Lall et al 2006). Probably, the most important contribution of the new mi-gration economics is to provide new insights into the po-tentially positive link between internal migration and ru-ral development, stressing the role of remittances sent to the rural area. The implications of these models are that policies should ensure that the role played by migration on rural development is optimized. This covers a multi-tude of potential interventions and programmes, such as helping migrants send remittances home, helping rural recipients allocate remittances for rural development by directing them towards more productive investment and even interventions that enable migrants to keep social links with their areas of origin, not just to transfer remit-tances but also to transfer other resources such as infor-mation. An interesting implication of these models is that policies could also target those among poor families who do not have migrant members given that these families have less opportunities to escape poverty. For instance, micro-finance programmes in rural area could target non-migrant household who do not receive remittances to off-set credit constraints or who may not have access to the networks that provide services to wealthier migrant households. These works equally suggest that education or social net-works can help migrants in their gradual process of labour - market assimilation in the city even though the specific channels through which networks and education operate are not identified. These channels probably include differ-ent kinds of support or opportunities that migrants may lack. In this respect, policies that could help migrants overcome assimilation barriers are diverse. Measures can aim at improving the bargaining power of migrants on the labour-market, for instance through sharing information and collective negotiation (mosse et al, 2002). Govern-ment can see to it that migrants, and especially poor mi-grants, are not credit rationed so that entrepreneurship is not discouraged, that migrants, are not discriminated against in the housing market, and that they find satisfac-tory access to public goods, including child care, health and education. Supporting the political involvement of migrants in local politics can help migrants ensure that their needs are taken into account by local policy makers. Several other measures can ensure the protection of rural migrants in cities, providing them with information on 27 labour legislation and rights, favoring their unionization, and ensuring that they hold written contracts. Welfare funds can provide insurance to the most vulnerable migrants. Oth-er measure can guarantee to preserve the rights of migrants in the rural area of origin while away (lall et al 2006). The inflow of migrants to cities can be problematic if not accommodated properly. Accommodationist policies should be designed to cover programmes to assist migrants upon their arrival in urban areas in order to reduce the economic and social costs of migration and to maximize its gains. The idea is to provide rural migrants with an environment that allows them to make use of their energy and skills ( skeldon, 1997). Measures can be as diverse as the creation of recep-tion centers for migrants, helping them access essential pub-lic goods, or helping them acquire affordable housing. It is worthy to note that already, policies in developing countries are increasingly more concerned with influencing the direction of rural to urban migrants flows-e.g to particu-lar areas-with the implicit understanding that migration will occur anyway and thus should be accommodated at as low a cost as possible. enriched_points.expanded_content: Migration patterns can be understood through theories like the Harris-Todaro model, which illustrates the economic motivations behind rural-urban migration and its implications.
Policies should focus on balancing rural development and urban job creation to mitigate the adverse effects of migration on food security and local economies.
Challenges such as labor shortages in agriculture and urban unemployment necessitate targeted interventions that support both rural livelihoods and urban integration for migrants.
